question: Does the 5-HTT (5-HTTLPR) polymorphism moderate the effect of stressful life events on risk of depression?
title: Influence of life stress on depression: moderation by a polymorphism in the 5-HTT gene.
text: In a prospective-longitudinal study of a representative birth cohort, we tested why stressful experiences lead to depression in some people but not in others. A functional polymorphism in the promoter region of the serotonin transporter (5-HT T) gene was found to moderate the influence of stressful life events on depression. Individuals with one or two copies of the short allele of the 5-HT T promoter polymorphism exhibited more depressive symptoms, diagnosable depression, and suicidality in relation to stressful life events than individuals homozygous for the long allele. This epidemiological study thus provides evidence of a gene-by-environment interaction, in which an individual's response to environmental insults is moderated by his or her genetic makeup.

finding.value: 5-HTTLPR short allele carriers show stronger stress-depression link than long/long
finding.quote: Individuals with one or two copies of the short allele of the 5-HT T promoter polymorphism exhibited more depressive symptoms, diagnosable depression, and suicidality in relation to stressful life events than individuals homozygous for the long allele.

model.quote: prospective-longitudinal study of a representative birth cohort

intervention.value: Naturalistic exposure to stressful life events (not experimental)
intervention.quote: we tested why stressful experiences lead to depression in some people but not in others

assay.value: Genotyping of 5-HTT promoter polymorphism; depression/suicidality assessment
assay.quote: A functional polymorphism in the promoter region of the serotonin transporter (5-HT T) gene

endpoint.value: Depressive symptoms, diagnosable depression, and suicidality
endpoint.quote: exhibited more depressive symptoms, diagnosable depression, and suicidality in relation to stressful life events

statistic.quote: This epidemiological study thus provides evidence of a gene-by-environment interaction, in which an individual's response to environmental insults is moderated by his or her genetic makeup.
